item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry this didn't work for you. These are legitimate critiques, though I'm currently unsure if changing all these things would make the work better so much as different. I understand the reaction you had at the end; my intent was to get across that all this "morally justified great evil" was, in the end, pointless and absurd, as was any attempt at a narrative that placed any character in the story as 'heroic' (or even relevant), but I'm not sure I fully pinned that down.

Perhaps that's also a reading order issue, or more likely, that I tried to do too much in this article. I still hope it works, of course, but perhaps the final log should have been in another story, or perhaps there is still a missing piece. Hard to say.

I wanted to comment on one minor thing:

Something about the clinical tone feels off, in a way that's noticeable but hard to describe.

I can't claim the tone or style here is perfect (I think this SCP is overly wordy, for starters, and when something is overly wordy, the tone very well may be off too). But I've seen comments like this crop up multiple times over the years, and my impression is this: for years now, critiques of 'clinical tone' have drifted from addressing actual clinical tone to trying to match a certain modern SCP 'house style'.

(Here's an example of discussion about the topic from 2016, where I felt tone and style were being conflated. Most of discussion about this issue took place on chat, sadly, where it is now Forever Lost.)

I deliberately don't use that modern 'house style' because I find it less enjoyable to read (though there's nothing outright wrong with it). I've found that works that don't use that style tend to get critiqued for 'tone' when the issue isn't tone at all, but using a style the reader doesn't favor.

I think this is a shame, because sometimes there is a problem there, and the reader can't diagnose it because they're defaulting to 'tone'. (Not necessarily in this case, but a general observation.)

After all this, perhaps the tone of this SCP is off. We are always the least objective judges of our own work, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading this was honestly kind of a drag. Something about the clinical tone feels off, in a way that's noticeable but hard to describe. The exploration logs weren't gripping or interesting, with the "multiple point of views" thing feeling like a hackneyed attempt of cramming cool imagery in. I haven't actually read 1985, but the Extra-Universal Investigation log just feels like squandering it, since not a single entry was compelling to read. The "twist" that there's a mainline reality doesn't quite gel with how this is after the 001 in the hub's reading order.

Finally, the massive exposition dump at the just kills the pacing, even rendering the fragments just before it superfluous. I don't like the twist here either, as it completely reframes the entire series… but not in a way that's interesting or ambitious. Okay, it turns out the whole Big Bad threat is just some rock from some war between interdimensional beings. It hypes up the size of the things fighting too much, that it feels downright silly, rather than menacing and frightful. Like, an attempt at Lovecraftian "Reality is so big we're so small" horror that comes across as a kid in the playground saying "My dad is bigger than your dad".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, this skip is really, really cool, it deserves much more attention. It's somewhat similar to a narrative I'm trying to write through multiple SCPs, but this is so much bigger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone else mentioned Worm to me when I was finishing up WMDD's Proposal (connected to this piece). I haven't read it, but maybe I should!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of eden from worm the web serial

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic and deserves more upvotes than I have. The physical descriptions are wonderfully evocative without compromising the essential mystery of the object, and the attempts to describe it in terms of its meaning create a sense of enormous implied meaning and scale. This one thing credibly implies a whole context in which we might as well be microorganisms colonizing one particular pebble, complete with threats so big that they haven't noticed we exist and might kill us anyway.

The connection to the contest entry feels solid, and yet ancillary enough to the article that I would feel comfortable reading about 3591 without investigating the entire entry- in short, a perfect crossover tie-in. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, despite not liking the proposal at all, I did in fact quite enjoy this. I wonder if there's a way to more smoothly incorporate the links to the proposal after the contest, or otherwise just intertwine that in here. (Hell, this almost stands on its own. Almost.) This was a very fun expectations-twisting read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I usually dislike all these doomsday and doomsday style artifacts, because they declare things within their specific canon that are so permanently world-changing that I feel like they belong more as tales about the object, than an object themselves.

But I personally felt this one was gorgeous. Loved it, +1. I feel the whole sense of doom without it being outright established that this will end the world. In fact, it feels like this could easily never actually be a world ending problem.

But it sure is a spooky existential one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like, why is the size described in terms of what it could crush? That's… just not a thing.

Because their other measurements are all off. The only thing that they are 100% sure of is that it can and did crush a specific area of ███-███ square kilometers.

After the contest, I'll add a note about that!

This SCP was mostly written in a single all-nighter (my IRL schedule really sucks right now), and after I've spent the last few years working on mainstream fiction in a casual voice, so any feedback on tonal issues would be very helpful.

I particularly like that last unique reality that said it was baseline (I have to imagine that's where the last fellow came from)

It is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of things not to like about this, almost all of them minor tonal issues.

Massive physical size, sufficient to crush an area of ███-███ square kilometers.

Like, why is the size described in terms of what it could crush? That's… just not a thing.

But there's really a ton to like. This higher-dimensional dead thing that we can't identify beyond "yep, it's dead". The Foundation's preoccupation with understanding things they never can. I particularly like that last unique reality that said it was baseline (I have to imagine that's where the last fellow came from). On that note, I'm shocked 1985 hasn't been showing up again and again this contest. Using her was a great idea.

So yeah, +1 in total.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
found a typo in the "Head Test":

While many of the appearances are are strange

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God its been a while since I've read some good ol' exploration logs. And these were quite nice ones. If you were trying to emanate SCP-093 this does that quite well. Part of me kind of wished that the ending revelation was foreshadowed a tad more by the preceding logs, but that's me being super nit picky.

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
SCP article entry to the Doomsday Contest. A Fallen Giant.

While it may not seem like it until closer to the end, this piece is intrinsically linked to our proposal for SCP-001.

Special thanks to weizhong, Drewbear, and Dexanote.
